content.full_text:
October 22, 1991

John Wallace  
Fair Political Practice Commission  
428 J Street, Suite 800  
P.O. Box 807  
Sacramento, CA. 95804-0807

Re: Financing Homart/Councilman Penna

Dear John:

This letter will confirm our discussion of today's date wherein the potential conflict of South San Francisco Councilman John Penna was discussed.

The particular facts I related to you are as follows:

Mr. Penna is a licensed real estate broker whose office is located in South San Francisco. He also serves as a member of the City Council and Redevelopment Agency. In two opinion letters from your office (August 7, 1991 and October 12, 1990), it was determined that Councilman Penna should be disqualified from participating in decisions establishing a particular redevelopment project area and major decisions concerning that area which have a foreseeable material effect on his real estate business.

The August 7th letter also points out that certain decisions may be "severable" and therefore not pose a conflict situation. Hence, the letter cautions that each project must be analyzed separately to determine if the Councilman may participate.

The situation which gave rise to my call involves the financing, or refinancing, of obligations of the Redevelopment Agency under an owner participation agreement. The agreement is already in place and the obligation for repayment already exists. The only issue to be addressed by the Council is financing alternatives.

In discussions with Councilman Penna, he assured me that he had no financial interest in the project or in any potential bonding companies and/or banks which may be involved in the financing. Moreover, neither the Councilman nor myself could foresee how the

choice of financing mechanism for an existing obligation would have a material effect on his current or future business.

Subsequent to our initial discussion of this matter, you forwarded to me two advice letters dealing with "implementing decisions". (April 14, 1986 and September 14, 1989). After reviewing the letters, we had a second discussion wherein you indicated that the facts related to you appeared to present a situation similar to the above-cited letters. In addition, you expressed your belief that the current situation may be the type of implementing decision which would not have an effect on the Councilman's business and thus not preclude his participation.

Based upon my conversations with Councilman Penna and discussions with you, I have advised the Councilman that he may participate and vote on the choice of financing techniques for an existing obligation as long as it does not present a foreseeable material benefit to his real estate business.

Thank you for your cooperation and assistance in this matter.

Very truly yours,

MICHAEL J. O'TOOLE
City Attorney

MJO/mm

cc: Councilman John Penna
cc: Jesús Armas, City Manager
